title: Welcome to Premed

objectives.statement: Describe the recommended Premed subject journey and explain at least two ways one stage prepares the next.
Explain why representative concepts from the Premed subjects matter for later medical study.
Identify the scientific knowledge and concrete capabilities learners should be able to use by course completion.

scenes.title: Welcome to Premed
scenes.contentHtml:
Welcome to Premed

Welcome to Premed. You are about to begin a journey through the science that makes the human body more understandable and scientific evidence more trustworthy.

You do not need to arrive knowing the subjects below. This welcome shows you what you will learn, why each subject earns its place, and what you will be able to do when the pieces come together. The course begins with careful questions, moves through matter and living systems, and ends with you using evidence to explain unfamiliar problems.

Here is the journey ahead.

  • Scientific method and laboratory practice: You will turn curiosity into testable questions, choose variables and fair comparisons, measure carefully, keep reproducible records, and decide what a result supports. These habits matter throughout medical science because evidence must be open to inspection and challenge.
  • Mathematics and statistics: Units, ratios, graphs, probability, averages, variation, and ranges that express uncertainty will help you read research, compare groups without hiding variation, check plausible answers, and distinguish a numerical pattern from a justified conclusion.
  • Physics: Forces and motion; pressure, fluids, and transport; electricity; waves, sound, and light; heat; and radiation help explain movement, blood flow, electrical signals, hearing, vision, measurement, and imaging.
  • General chemistry: Atoms, bonds, molecular shape, reactions, amounts, gases, solutions, energy changes, reaction rates, equilibrium, acids, and bases describe the molecular setting of cells and supply language and calculations for laboratory work, biology, and biochemistry.
  • Organic chemistry: You will see how molecular shape and recurring groups of atoms influence carbon-based molecules, how electrons move in reactions, why three-dimensional arrangement matters, and how separation and instrument patterns reveal structure. This prepares you to reason about why molecules of life bind, change, and behave differently.
  • Biochemistry: Proteins and enzymes, membranes, carbohydrates, fats, nucleotides, metabolism—the linked reactions that manage energy and materials—and biological information come together here. You will use them to explain how cells obtain and spend energy, regulate reactions, communicate, and respond to molecular change.
  • Biology and genetics: Cell organization, membranes, division, signalling, inheritance, gene expression, variation, and evolution take you from molecules to living systems: how cells preserve information, develop different structures and functions, and generate variation across individuals and populations.
  • Human structure and physiology: You will connect tissues and organs with their functions, then examine transport, control of internal conditions, nerves, hormones, movement, circulation, breathing, fluid balance, digestion, reproduction, and development. The aim is to explain whole-body mechanisms, not make clinical decisions.
  • Microbiology and immunology: By comparing microbes and viruses, growth, transmission, evolution, barriers, rapid general defences, and slower targeted responses, you will explain infection and immunity as interactions among an agent, a host, an environment, and time.
  • Behaviour, society, and population health: Perception, learning, stress, social relationships, institutions, inequality, access, environment, and population evidence widen the account of health. Biology cannot explain every pattern, and a group association does not automatically explain one person's experience.
  • Integration and capstone work: Finally, you will bring subjects together around an unfamiliar, non-diagnostic problem; inspect sources and data; calculate or model; explain mechanisms; communicate uncertainty; and revise after critique. The goal is a defensible scientific explanation, not a pile of separate facts.

The destination is demanding but concrete. By completion, you should be able to explain important scientific relationships, model them with words, quantities, diagrams, or data, investigate questions with honest methods and records, and integrate several subjects when one alone is not enough.

For today, notice the promise inside that destination: nobody expects you to begin at the end. Curiosity, careful practice, and a willingness to correct your reasoning are enough to start.

Source Note
claims claim-subject-scope, claim-subject-preparation, claim-completion-capabilitiessources source-premed-syllabus

Adapted under CC BY 4.0 from the Premed syllabus's subject breadth, preparation, and course destination.

scenes.title: How the subjects prepare you for what follows
scenes.contentHtml:
How the subjects prepare you for what follows

The recommended journey is chronological, although some subjects run together. Its order gives you ideas for later explanations while showing where the subjects meet.

  1. Begin with scientific inquiry, laboratory practice, mathematics, and statistics. You learn to frame a question, compare fairly, measure with units, read graphs, describe variation, and preserve an honest record. The same habits let you test a chemical prediction, interpret a genetics result, or challenge a population claim.
  2. Study general chemistry and introductory biology side by side. Chemistry explains particles, bonds, reactions, solutions, and energy. Biology introduces molecules, membranes, cells, transport, and organization. Together they connect a visible biological outcome with the particles and interactions that could produce it.
  3. Add physics while chemistry and biology deepen. Forces, energy, pressure, flow, electricity, waves, and light make measurements and body processes easier to model. Equilibrium, acids and bases, inheritance, and gene expression add regulation and continuity. These subjects help you test whether an explanation is chemically, physically, and biologically possible.
  4. Use organic chemistry to enter biochemistry. Carbon-based structures, functional groups, three-dimensional shape, and reaction patterns prepare you to reason about proteins, membranes, nucleic acids, and metabolism. Biochemistry then shows how those molecules cooperate in a regulated cell and how evidence can test the explanation.
  5. Carry those foundations into human structure, physiology, microbiology, and immunology. Tissues and organs depend on transport, energy, signalling, and control. Infection and defence depend on cells, molecules, evolution, and population change. You now use what you learned earlier to explain how the whole body functions.
  6. Broaden the account with behaviour, society, and population health. People live in social and physical environments, so later medical study needs evidence about behaviour, institutions, access, inequality, and populations. This helps you distinguish a molecular mechanism, an individual experience, and a population pattern.
  7. Finish with integration and capstone work. A new problem will not announce which subject to use. You must choose relevant ideas, connect them through cause or evidence, inspect limitations, and communicate a conclusion another person can audit.
Check
id check-sequence-why

Cover the list and answer in one sentence: why does organic chemistry come before much of biochemistry, and why do inquiry and quantitative reasoning begin before both?

A useful answer says that organic chemistry prepares you to reason about carbon-based structures and reactions, while inquiry and quantitative reasoning provide methods for testing, measuring, and interpreting claims. If you repeated only the order, explain why each step helps.

The pace can vary, and you will revisit earlier ideas. The broad path is to learn how evidence works, build from matter to cells, extend the explanation to people and populations, then use it in an unfamiliar problem.

Source Note
claims claim-recommended-sequence, claim-subject-preparationsources source-premed-syllabus

Adapted under CC BY 4.0 from the syllabus's recommended sequence and prerequisites; institutions may order subjects differently.

scenes.title: What learning will look like
scenes.contentHtml:
What learning will look like

You will not learn this science by reading definitions once. Study asks you to meet an explanation, retrieve it without looking, solve or model something, inspect evidence, receive feedback, and try again with less support.

Practical work may be a physical investigation, a simulation, or work with a supplied dataset or observation. All three can build question-design and analysis skills, but only physical work can show that you performed a hands-on action. Your course record will name which kind you completed, and this welcome requires no physical work.

Here is a preview using a fictional training dataset. It requires no prior science.

Worked Example
id worked-example-flow-previewlabel A model-flow question from start to finish

Task. A simulation pumps the same liquid for ten seconds through two equal-length tubes. Tube A is narrow and Tube B is wider.

Run Tube A Tube B
1 20 mL 79 mL
2 19 mL 82 mL
3 21 mL 78 mL

The question is: Does tube width affect the volume collected in this model?

Represent what is given. Width is the planned difference. Liquid, tube length, pump setting, and collection time are held constant. The result is volume after ten seconds.

Choose a principle and plan. A fair comparison changes the tested feature while keeping the other conditions the same. Compare all three runs, then keep the conclusion inside the simulation.

Work through the evidence. Tube A stays near 20 millilitres and Tube B near 80. Every Tube B result is about four times its paired Tube A result. The difference between tubes is much larger than the small run-to-run variation.

Check the reasoning. Units and collection times match, and no run reverses the pattern. We should still confirm that the simulation held the pump and liquid constant; otherwise width would not be the only planned explanation.

Interpret and limit. The wider tube allowed more liquid to pass in this model under these settings. The data do not establish an exact law or tell us what happens in a human blood vessel. Later physics, chemistry, and physiology add pressure, resistance, fluids, tissues, and biological regulation.

Self-explanation. Which choice protects the conclusion most: collecting a large number, using millilitres, or holding the other conditions steady? Why?

The strongest answer is holding the other conditions steady, because that makes width the planned difference. Units make the measurement interpretable and repetition reveals variation, but neither fixes a comparison in which the pump or liquid also changed.

Check
id check-faded-comparison

A second dataset compares a narrow tube at a low pump setting with a wide tube at a high setting. Can it isolate the effect of width? Answer yes or no, name the competing difference, and propose a repair.

After answering, compare: No. Pump setting changes with width, so either could explain the result. Repeat the comparison at one pump setting. If you named only the problem, add the repaired comparison.

This is the course's reasoning pattern in miniature: define the question, choose relevant ideas, work carefully, check, interpret, and state limits.

Source Note
claims claim-learning-practice, claim-completion-capabilitiessources source-premed-syllabus

Adapted under CC BY 4.0 from the syllabus's practical modalities and learning practices; the table is an original fictional example.

scenes.title: The destination and your first check
scenes.contentHtml:
The destination and your first check

By the end of Premed, knowing the subjects means being able to use them. You should be able to:

  • Explain relationships from molecules to cells, organs, behaviour, and populations with accurate cause-and-evidence language.
  • Model a relationship with words, quantities, graphs, diagrams, chemical structures, or data, while stating what the representation leaves out.
  • Investigate a testable question with suitable measurements and comparisons, an auditable record, and a conclusion no stronger than the evidence.
  • Integrate several subjects in an unfamiliar problem, distinguish mechanism from association, communicate clearly, and revise after evidence or critique.

Premed prepares you for further study. It does not authorize diagnosis or treatment, award university credit or a professional licence, or guarantee admission.

Check
id check-welcome-transfer

A friend asks, “Why isn't Premed just biology?” Without looking back, give a brief answer that:

  1. names the subjects that begin, develop, and complete the journey;
  2. gives one example of a concept preparing later medical study; and
  3. names two actions a finisher should perform.

Use sentences or an outline. Do not teach a mechanism you have not learned.

A strong answer begins with inquiry and quantitative work; includes chemical, physical, biological, human, microbial, behavioural, social, and population subjects; and ends with capstone work. It also explains one preparation link—for example, pressure and flow prepare you to explain circulation. If you gave only a list, explain why one transition matters. If you discussed only the body, add how evidence is tested.

Misconception
id misconception-prior-science-gate

Worry: “If I cannot already explain enzymes, electricity, inheritance, or immunity, I am not ready.”

Repair: this welcome asks you to describe the journey, not perform untaught science. Place an unfamiliar term in its subject and say what the subject will unlock. For enzyme regulation, “Biochemistry will teach how cells control reaction helpers called enzymes” is enough today.

The welcome check

The welcome check has three untimed text responses worth ten points. To complete it, score at least 8 out of 10 and earn at least one point on each response. You may retake it; it checks your orientation, not your prior science.

On a retry, use genuinely different prompts:

  • advise an incoming learner how to travel from trustworthy evidence to the capstone;
  • choose different subjects or concepts and explain what each unlocks;
  • apply the four capabilities to a new non-diagnostic capstone scenario.

Accessibility and alternatives

Choose paragraphs, bullets, or an outline. You can type, paste text from assistive technology, or use device dictation in a text field; direct audio upload is not available. No task asks for personal health information, speed, sight alone, or physical practical work. Headings and lists keep a semantic reading order for keyboard, screen-reader, reflow, zoom, narrow-screen, and print use, and the table's meaning is also explained in words.

Recovery route

If the names blur together, start again with three stages: evidence and measurement; matter and living systems; people, populations, and capstone work. Add the named subjects and complete: “I will learn ___ in ___ because it helps me later explain or evaluate ___.” Check the glossary when you need it, then try again from memory.

Source Note
claims claim-subject-scope, claim-learning-practice, claim-completion-capabilities, claim-orientation-boundarysources source-premed-syllabus

Adapted under CC BY 4.0 from the syllabus's capabilities, alternatives, recovery, and boundaries; the threshold and retry prompts are original to this welcome.

items.prompt: Describe the recommended Premed subject journey to a new learner. Begin with scientific method and laboratory practice plus mathematics and statistics; include general chemistry, physics, organic chemistry, biochemistry, biology and genetics, human structure and physiology, microbiology and immunology, behaviour, society, and population health, and integration and capstone work. Explain at least two ways one stage prepares the next.

answer.correct: A complete response begins with inquiry, laboratory, and quantitative practices; moves through the chemical, physical, biological, and molecular foundations; carries them into human systems, microbes, immunity, behaviour, society, and populations; and ends with integration and capstone work. It explains at least two preparation links rather than giving only an unordered list.
answer.summary: The journey moves from trustworthy evidence and measurement through matter and living systems to people, populations, and an unfamiliar capstone problem.
answer.reasoning: Inquiry, laboratory, mathematics, and statistics come first because every later subject uses measurements and evidence. General chemistry, biology, and physics provide particle, cell, and physical explanations. Organic chemistry prepares molecular reasoning used in biochemistry; these foundations then support physiology, microbiology, immunity, and behavioural or population contexts. Integration and capstone work require selecting and connecting the relevant foundations. On retry, advise an incoming learner how to proceed from trustworthy evidence to a capstone rather than reproducing the original list.
acceptableResponses: Prose, bullets, or an outline that names every required subject area, preserves the broad recommended chronology, and explains at least two accurate preparation relationships.
commonErrors: Giving an unordered subject list with no explanation of why the order helps
Omitting behaviour, society, population health, or integration and capstone work
Beginning with advanced medical content instead of evidence and measurement

items.prompt: Choose four representative concepts from four different Premed subject areas. In plain language, explain what each concept helps unlock for later medical study. You are describing relevance, not teaching the detailed science.

answer.correct: The response identifies four representative concepts from four different subject areas and gives a defensible later-study link for each, such as uncertainty to research interpretation, pressure and flow to circulation, molecular shape to biomolecule behaviour, gene expression to cell differences, or social conditions to population health patterns.
answer.summary: Representative concepts earn their place by enabling later explanations, measurements, or evidence judgments.
answer.reasoning: This task asks for a concept-to-purpose relationship. Naming a subject is not enough; each example must say what the concept later helps explain or evaluate. A retry should use different subjects or concepts so that the learner reconstructs the relationship instead of copying feedback.
acceptableResponses: Any four accurate concept-and-relevance pairs drawn from four different named subject areas in the lesson.
commonErrors: Listing concepts without saying what they unlock
Making clinical claims instead of describing preparation for study
Using four concepts from only one subject

items.prompt: A learner says that completing Premed means remembering a large set of facts. Correct that account. State the four main completion capabilities and give one concrete action that shows each capability.

answer.correct: Completion requires explaining scientific relationships, modelling them with appropriate representations, investigating questions with auditable methods, and integrating several subjects in unfamiliar problems. Each capability must be tied to an observable action.
answer.summary: Premed aims at explanation, modelling, investigation, and integration rather than recall alone.
answer.reasoning: Facts support the four capabilities but do not replace them. Concrete evidence includes explaining a relationship, using and limiting a graph or model, designing and documenting a fair investigation, and connecting several domains in a new problem. On retry, apply the four capabilities to a new non-diagnostic capstone scenario rather than repeating their definitions.
acceptableResponses: Equivalent wording that identifies explain, model, investigate, and integrate and gives an observable example of each.
commonErrors: Treating course completion as recall or time spent
Naming the four verbs without concrete actions
Claiming that completion grants clinical competence or professional authority

criteria.description: Begins with scientific method, laboratory practice, mathematics, and statistics as shared foundations.
Includes the chemical, physical, biological, genetic, organic, and biochemical subjects in a defensible broad order.
Includes human structure and physiology, microbiology and immunology, behaviour, society, population health, and integration and capstone work.
Explains at least two accurate ways one stage prepares later study.
Selects four representative concepts from four different subject areas.
Explains an accurate later-study purpose for each selected concept.
Uses plain language and stays at orientation depth without unsupported clinical claims.
Identifies explaining, modelling, investigating, and integrating.
Gives one observable scientific action for each capability.
Distinguishes scientific preparation from recall alone and from clinical or professional authority.

sources.title: Premed: Foundations for Medical Study

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: The lesson intentionally retains and adapts the syllabus's named subject sequence and its explain, model, investigate, and integrate destination. Full attribution, a CC BY 4.0 link, and a modification notice are provided in ATTRIBUTION.md; the lesson adds original teaching prose, examples, prompts, recovery guidance, and assessment design.

agentAccess.notes: The source was read from the local authorized repository checkout. Its repository content licence expressly permits sharing and adaptation with attribution and change notice, and no source-specific term prohibiting this agent access was found on the exact local route used.

claims.statement: The Premed syllabus includes scientific method and laboratory practice; mathematics and statistics; physics; general chemistry; organic chemistry; biochemistry; biology and genetics; human structure and physiology; microbiology and immunology; behaviour, society, and population health; and integration and capstone work.
claims.scope: EmbeddedKnowledge Premed curriculum version 1.1 candidate, including its common core, medicine-facing bridge, route-specific behavioural material or supplied primer, and integration work.
claims.uncertainty: Behavioural and social depth varies by selected route; the welcome names the subject area without presenting every route-specific module as portable common core.

claims.statement: Within the recommended Premed journey, inquiry and quantitative reasoning support later evidence use; chemical, physical, biological, organic, and biochemical foundations prepare later work with human systems, infection, immunity, behaviour, populations, and unfamiliar capstone problems.
claims.scope: Instructional dependencies and medicine-facing uses declared in the EmbeddedKnowledge syllabus, not universal admissions prerequisites.
claims.uncertainty: Some modules may run concurrently, and institution-specific prerequisite sequences can differ.

claims.statement: The recommended sequence begins with the welcome and scientific inquiry, develops chemistry, biology, and physics, then organic chemistry and biochemistry, continues through a medicine-facing bridge and a selected route, and ends with integration and capstone work.
claims.scope: Recommended module order in EmbeddedKnowledge Premed curriculum version 1.1 candidate.
claims.uncertainty: The syllabus permits concurrent modules and pace variation after shared prerequisites.

claims.statement: Premed uses explanation, retrieval, modelling, problem solving, physical or simulated investigations, supplied datasets or observations, feedback, retry, and accessibility alternatives; only physical work demonstrates hands-on actions, so practical modality is reported.
claims.scope: Course learning design, practical curriculum, accessibility provisions, and recovery rules in the syllabus.
claims.uncertainty: The exact available physical activities and accommodations depend on delivery context and approved safety conditions.

claims.statement: The Premed destination is to explain, model, investigate, and integrate foundational science, using evidence and communicating limits in unfamiliar but bounded problems.
claims.scope: The course promise, program-level outcomes, WEL-000 purpose, and capstone destination in the syllabus.
claims.uncertainty: This is the intended curricular destination; demonstrated completion still depends on later course evidence and does not follow from finishing the welcome lesson.

claims.statement: The welcome is a motivating orientation rather than a prior-science gate, and Premed completion does not itself grant university credit, admission, clinical competence, or professional authority.
claims.scope: WEL-000 completion evidence and syllabus completion interpretation.

terms.definition: An evidence-building process that frames testable questions, compares explanations with observations, and revises conclusions when the evidence requires it; it is not a single rigid recipe.
Methods for describing variation in data and reasoning carefully from observations to bounded conclusions.
A purposeful representation of selected features and relationships used to explain, predict, or test an idea within stated limits.
The study of biological inheritance, variation, genes, and how genetic information contributes to observable traits.
The study of the molecules and chemical processes of living systems, including enzymes, membranes, energy use, and biological information.
The study of how living structures function and regulate their internal conditions across cells, tissues, organs, and systems.
The study of microscopic organisms and related biological entities, including how they grow, change, and interact with hosts and environments.
The study of biological defence, including barriers, rapid responses, targeted responses, tolerance, and immune memory.
The study of health patterns and their distribution across groups, together with the biological, behavioural, social, and environmental conditions related to those patterns.
A culminating task in which a learner selects and connects knowledge from several subjects to address an unfamiliar, bounded problem and defend the reasoning.

attributionHtml:
Attribution

Lesson authorship and licence

Welcome to Premed (PREM-WEL-001), version 0.1.0, was prepared for EmbeddedKnowledge under the accountable principal VSBDev (github:VSBDev). Except where stated below, the lesson prose, fictional training data, prompts, assessment, glossary, and structured records are offered under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International licence.

No third-party image, audio, video, software, or dataset is included in this pack.

Adapted curriculum source

This lesson adapts EmbeddedKnowledge, Premed: Foundations for Medical Study, curriculum version 1.1 candidate (20 July 2026) (source-premed-syllabus), available in the EmbeddedKnowledge repository and licensed under CC BY 4.0.

Material retained and adapted: the syllabus's named subject sequence, the relationship among successive stages of study, its physical/simulation/dataset practical modalities, and its four-part destination of explaining, modelling, investigating, and integrating science.

Modifications: the syllabus material was reorganized and rewritten as an original 60-minute, four-scene welcome for a novice learner. The adaptation separates the subjects into an explicit plain-language overview, explains how each subject prepares later medical study, supplies a fictional worked dataset, adds retrieval and transfer prompts, creates an original low-stakes assessment and retry forms, and adds learner-visible recovery and accessibility guidance. No syllabus question, table, example, media, or dataset was copied.

Agent assistance and provenance

Codex materially authored the draft under the accountability of VSBDev with this disclosed provenance:

  • System: Codex
  • Provider: OpenAI
  • Model: GPT-5.6-Sol
  • Version: Codex CLI 0.144.6
  • Run ID: author-codex-gpt56sol-20260720-0f9f6376-9e64-4002-8ae7-e7bc8759713d
  • Material-instructions digest: sha256:c2253cdb9007d71ebbfc765124f48a1768049b12ca05c4739b767192661ca8a1

The SHA-256 digest covers the exact discloseable material author prompt supplied to this run. It does not claim to cover hidden provider or system instructions that were not exportable as part of that material prompt.

This revision digest covers the exact discloseable revision prompt supplied to the run and excludes hidden provider instructions.

This pack remains a draft. It contains no review or adjudication artifact and makes no claim of approval, publication, course coverage, learner effectiveness, clinical sufficiency, academic credit, or admission eligibility.
